questions.question: Define searching. Classify different searching techniques and explain when each is appropriate. (5 marks)
keyPoints: Searching: Process of finding a specific element in a collection.
Linear Search: Sequential, O(n), works on unsorted/sorted data.
Binary Search: Divide and conquer, O(log n), requires sorted data.
Use linear for small/unsorted data; binary for large sorted data.

questions.question: Compare linear search and binary search in terms of working principle, time complexity, space complexity, and prerequisites. (5 marks)
keyPoints: Linear: Checks each element sequentially. Binary: Compares with middle, eliminates half.
Linear: O(n) worst. Binary: O(log n) worst.
Both use O(1) space iteratively. Binary recursive uses O(log n) stack space.
Linear: No prerequisite. Binary: Requires sorted data and random access.

questions.question: Write C programs for linear search and binary search. Trace through an example for each. (10 marks)
keyPoints: Linear search C code: Loop through array, compare each element with key.
Binary search C code: Set low=0, high=n-1, compute mid, compare and adjust bounds.
Linear trace example: Searching for 7 in [3, 8, 1, 7, 5] - found at index 3.
Binary trace example: Searching for 7 in [1, 3, 5, 7, 8] - mid comparisons shown.
Handle not-found case for both algorithms.

questions.question: Explain the advantages and limitations of binary search. (5 marks)
keyPoints: Advantage: Very efficient O(log n), suitable for large datasets.
Advantage: Eliminates half the search space in each step.
Limitation: Requires sorted data - sorting takes O(n log n).
Limitation: Requires random access - cannot be used directly on linked lists.
